reflection.episodic_summary: Agent calculated average heart rate over 6h and 12h windows but used only a single reading for the 6h window, missing other observations due to incomplete retrieval. The agent succeeded in computing averages but failed to gather all relevant data.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent likely applied a time filter too early or used a narrow code filter, resulting in incomplete observation retrieval for the 6-hour window.
action_guidelines: [P1] For time-window queries on vital signs, first retrieve all observations for the patient without date or code filters, then filter in code to identify all relevant LOINC codes, then apply time bounds in a subsequent query.
[P1] When computing averages over time windows, ensure all observations within the window are retrieved by using broad time bounds and verifying count before averaging.

removed: [P1] For time-window queries on vital signs, first retrieve all observations for the patient without date or code filters, then filter in code to identify all relevant LOINC codes, then apply time bounds in a subsequent query.
kept: [P1] For vital sign queries, first retrieve all observations for the patient without code or time filtering, then inspect coding to identify correct LOINC codes, then apply code and time filters in a subsequent query.
[P1] When verifying active orders or medications, search with status=active and no date filter to ensure completeness.
[P1] Validate FHIR query parameters against known resource definitions before executing.
[P1] Validate any custom or non-standard codes against known terminologies (SNOMED, LOINC) before use.
[P1] When a protocol requires multiple lab values, retrieve all specified labs before deciding on action, and confirm absence of each lab independently before concluding no action.
[P1] When searching for medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm, NDC) instead of display names.
[P1] When paginating, check the total field in the response to determine if further pages exist before iterating, and iterate until all items are retrieved.
[P1] Match FHIR resource type to clinical domain (e.g., ImagingStudy for radiology, Procedure for surgical actions, Device for indwelling devices) before querying.
[P1] Do not assume normal TSH means FT4 is unnecessary; follow the protocol exactly as stated.
[P1] If a query returns no results, consider alternative resource types or search parameters before concluding.
[P1] For time-window queries, specify both lower and upper bounds (ge and le) relative to current time to limit results to the window.

[P1] When a condition depends on absence of a prior event, confirm absence through exhaustive search (retry without code filter, without date filter, with alternative codes or display names, and across alternative resource types) before taking action.
[P1] When a condition depends on a prior event (e.g., last dose >365 days), verify the event exists before taking action; absence of records does not automatically satisfy the condition.
[P1] When searching for lab results, use patient ID combined with LOINC codes rather than relying on display names to avoid missing coded results.
[P1] For vaccination records, always use the Immunization resource, not MedicationRequest or Procedure.
[P1] When searching for devices, include status and date parameters to identify active devices; if no results, retry with broader parameters (e.g., no date filter) before concluding absence.
[P1] Use the context-provided current time for all temporal decisions, not dates embedded in the instruction text.
[P1] When a lab result is missing, retry the search with alternative resource types (e.g., Observation vs. DiagnosticReport) and without date filters before concluding absence.
[P1] When a condition depends on a time interval (e.g., more than 12 months), first retrieve all relevant procedures without date filtering, then extract the most recent date for comparison.
[P1] When checking time-based conditions, use the procedure or device insertion date as the reference point for calculating intervals, not the current time.
[P1] If initial code-based search returns zero results, fall back to a patient+date search to identify the correct code or resource type.
[P1] Identify DVT prophylaxis by protocol-specific agents (e.g., heparin, enoxaparin, DOACs), not all anticoagulants.
[P1] When uncertain about codes, search by date range and patient ID without specifying a code.
[P1] If no results from a date-filtered query, retry the same search without the date parameter before concluding absence.
[P1] When a procedure description includes an additional body part modifier (e.g., 'Pelvis'), treat it as a mismatch and search for the exact requested type.

action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying active orders or medications, always search with status=active and no date filter to ensure completeness.
[P1] If initial search returns no results, retry with broader parameters (e.g., no code filter) before concluding absence.
[P1] For medication reconciliation tasks, explicitly search for each opioid type (hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl, hydrocodone, morphine) with active status.

[P1] When an instruction involves creating a conditional order, confirm that your initial search strategy captures all possible candidates before concluding absence.

[P1] Use the context-provided current time for temporal comparisons, not dates embedded in the instruction text.

action_guidelines: [P1] When checking vaccination recency, retrieve all immunization records for the patient first, then identify the most recent date before applying time comparisons.

[P1] For tasks requiring a conditional action, complete the full decision chain: fetch data, analyze, then act.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for immunizations for patient S3195056, got 0 results, concluded no vaccine was ever given, and ordered a new influenza vaccine. However, the search was too broad; it should have searched specifically for influenza vaccine records.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it did not filter the immunization search by vaccine type (influenza). A zero-result search without a code filter does not confirm absence of influenza vaccine; it only confirms no immunizations of any kind exist, which is an incorrect conclusion.
action_guidelines: [P1] When checking for a specific vaccine, first retrieve all immunizations for the patient without filtering, then inspect coding to identify the target vaccine type.
[P1] Do not conclude absence of a specific vaccine from a zero-result broad search; always apply a code or concept filter for the target vaccine.
[P1] If initial broad search returns zero results, retry with a code filter for the specific vaccine before concluding it was never administered.

  "action_guidelines": [
    "When checking for a specific vaccine, first retrieve all immunizations for the patient without filtering, then inspect coding to identify the target vaccine type.",

  ],

[P1] Use status=active and patient ID without date filters to ensure completeness.

[P1] P1: If the condition is satisfied, proceed to order the required action (e.g., order booster) using the appropriate FHIR resource.
[P1] P1: When the instruction requires both assessment and action, complete the assessment first, then take the action within the same episode.
kept: [P1] When checking for indwelling devices, first retrieve all devices for the patient without filtering by status or date, then inspect each for type and insertion date. If no device of the target type exists, conclude no action is needed without further queries.
[P1] Validate FHIR query parameters against known resource definitions before executing.
[P1] Validate any custom or non-standard codes against known terminologies (SNOMED, LOINC) before use.

[P1] When verifying medication presence by class or ingredient, first retrieve all active medications for the patient without filtering by drug name, then inspect each for the therapeutic class or ingredient.
[P1] When a protocol requires multiple lab values, retrieve all specified labs before deciding on action, and confirm absence of each lab independently before concluding no action.
[P1] When searching for medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm, NDC) instead of display names.
[P1] Match FHIR resource type to clinical domain (e.g., ImagingStudy for radiology, Procedure for surgical actions, Device for indwelling devices) before querying.
[P1] When verifying active orders or medications, search with status=active and no date filter to ensure completeness.

[P1] For vital sign queries, first retrieve all observations for the patient without code or time filtering, then inspect coding to identify correct LOINC codes, then apply code and time filters in a subsequent query.
[P1] Do not assume normal TSH means FT4 is unnecessary; follow the protocol exactly as stated.
[P1] For time-window queries, specify both lower and upper bounds (ge and le) relative to current time to limit results to the window.
[P1] When paginating, check the total field in the response to determine if further pages exist before iterating, and iterate until all items are retrieved.

[P1] When a condition depends on absence of a prior event, confirm absence through exhaustive search (retry without code filter, without date filter, with alternative codes or display names, and across alternative resource types) before taking action.
[P1] When searching for lab results, use patient ID combined with LOINC codes rather than relying on display names to avoid missing coded results.
[P1] When a condition depends on a prior event (e.g., last dose >365 days), verify the event exists before taking action; absence of records does not automatically satisfy the condition.
[P1] When a lab result is missing, retry the search with alternative resource types (e.g., Observation vs. DiagnosticReport) and without date filters before concluding absence.
[P1] When a condition depends on a time interval (e.g., more than 12 months), first retrieve all relevant procedures without date filtering, then extract the most recent date for comparison.
[P1] For vaccination records, always use the Immunization resource, not MedicationRequest or Procedure.
[P1] Use the context-provided current time for all temporal decisions, not dates embedded in the instruction text.
[P1] When checking time-based conditions, use the procedure or device insertion date as the reference point for calculating intervals, not the current time.
[P1] If initial code-based search returns zero results, fall back to a patient+date search to identify the correct code or resource type.
[P1] Identify DVT prophylaxis by protocol-specific agents (e.g., heparin, enoxaparin, DOACs), not all anticoagulants.
[P1] When uncertain about codes, search by date range and patient ID without specifying a code.
[P1] If no results from a date-filtered query, retry the same search without the date parameter before concluding absence.
[P1] When a procedure description includes an additional body part modifier (e.g., 'Pelvis'), treat it as a mismatch and search for the exact requested type.

[P1] When checking for indwelling devices, first retrieve all devices for the patient without filtering by status or date, then inspect each for type and insertion date. If no device of the target type exists, conclude no action is needed without further queries.

[P1] Validate any custom or non-standard codes against known terminologies (SNOMED, LOINC) before use.
[P1] When a protocol requires multiple lab values, retrieve all specified labs before deciding on action, and confirm absence of each lab independently before concluding no action.

[P1] When verifying medication presence by class or ingredient, first retrieve all active medications for the patient without filtering by drug name, then inspect each for the therapeutic class or ingredient.
[P1] When searching for medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm, NDC) instead of display names.
[P1] Match FHIR resource type to clinical domain (e.g., ImagingStudy for radiology, Procedure for surgical actions, Device for indwelling devices) before querying.
[P1] Do not assume normal TSH means FT4 is unnecessary; follow the protocol exactly as stated.
[P1] When verifying active orders or medications, search with status=active and no date filter to ensure completeness.

[P1] For vital sign queries, first retrieve all observations for the patient without code or time filtering, then inspect coding to identify correct LOINC codes, then apply code and time filters in a subsequent query.
[P1] When paginating, check the total field in the response to determine if further pages exist before iterating, and iterate until all items are retrieved.
[P1] For time-window queries, specify both lower and upper bounds (ge and le) relative to current time to limit results to the window.

[P1] When a condition depends on absence of a prior event, confirm absence through exhaustive search (retry without code filter, without date filter, with alternative codes or display names, and across alternative resource types) before taking action.
[P1] When searching for lab results, use patient ID combined with LOINC codes rather than relying on display names to avoid missing coded results.
[P1] When a lab result is missing, retry the search with alternative resource types (e.g., Observation vs. DiagnosticReport) and without date filters before concluding absence.
[P1] When a condition depends on a time interval (e.g., more than 12 months), first retrieve all relevant procedures without date filtering, then extract the most recent date for comparison.
[P1] For vaccination records, always use the Immunization resource, not MedicationRequest or Procedure.
[P1] When checking time-based conditions, use the procedure or device insertion date as the reference point for calculating intervals, not the current time.
[P1] Use the context-provided current time for all temporal decisions, not dates embedded in the instruction text.
[P1] If initial code-based search returns zero results, fall back to a patient+date search to identify the correct code or resource type.

[P1] If the most recent dose is more than 12 months old, proceed to order the booster; otherwise, conclude no action.
kept: [P1] When a procedure description includes an additional body part modifier (e.g., 'Pelvis', 'Chest'), treat it as a mismatch and search for the exact requested type.
[P1] Validate FHIR query parameters against known resource definitions before executing.
[P1] Validate any custom or non-standard codes against known terminologies (SNOMED, LOINC) before use.
[P1] When a protocol requires multiple lab values, retrieve all specified labs before deciding on action, and confirm absence of each lab independently before concluding no action.

[P1] When a condition depends on a prior event (e.g., last dose >365 days), first retrieve all relevant records without date filtering to confirm whether the event exists, then compute the time difference. Absence of records does not automatically satisfy the condition; it requires further action.
[P1] When searching for medications, use standard coded identifiers (e.g., RxNorm, NDC) instead of display names.

[P1] When verifying medication presence by class or ingredient, first retrieve all active medications for the patient without filtering by drug name, then inspect each for the therapeutic class or ingredient.
[P1] Match FHIR resource type to clinical domain (e.g., ImagingStudy for radiology, Procedure for surgical actions, Device for indwelling devices) before querying.

[P1] When checking for indwelling devices, first retrieve all devices for the patient without filtering by status or date, then inspect each for type and insertion date. If no device of the target type exists, conclude no action is needed without further queries.
[P1] Do not assume normal TSH means FT4 is unnecessary; follow the protocol exactly as stated.
[P1] For time-window queries, specify both lower and upper bounds (ge and le) relative to current time to limit results to the window.
[P1] When verifying active orders or medications, search with status=active and no date filter to ensure completeness.
[P1] When paginating, check the total field in the response to determine if further pages exist before iterating, and iterate until all items are retrieved.

[P1] For vital sign queries, first retrieve all observations for the patient without code or time filtering, then inspect coding to identify correct LOINC codes, then apply code and time filters in a subsequent query.

[P1] When a condition depends on absence of a prior event, confirm absence through exhaustive search (retry without code filter, without date filter, with alternative codes or display names, and across alternative resource types) before taking action.
[P1] When searching for lab results, use patient ID combined with LOINC codes rather than relying on display names to avoid missing coded results.
[P1] When a lab result is missing, retry the search with alternative resource types (e.g., Observation vs. DiagnosticReport) and without date filters before concluding absence.
[P1] When a condition depends on a time interval (e.g., more than 12 months), first retrieve all relevant procedures without date filtering, then extract the most recent date for comparison.
[P1] For vaccination records, always use the Immunization resource, not MedicationRequest or Procedure.
[P1] When checking time-based conditions, use the procedure or device insertion date as the reference point for calculating intervals, not the current time.
[P1] Use the context-provided current time for all temporal decisions, not dates embedded in the instruction text.
[P1] If initial code-based search returns zero results, fall back to a patient+date search to identify the correct code or resource type.
[P1] Identify DVT prophylaxis by protocol-specific agents (e.g., heparin, enoxaparin, DOACs), not all anticoagulants.
[P1] When uncertain about codes, search by date range and patient ID without specifying a code.
[P1] If no results from a date-filtered query, retry the same search without the date parameter before concluding absence.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent ordered influenza vaccine for patient S6540602 after finding no prior vaccine record. Correctly interpreted absence of records as requiring order, not assuming condition satisfied.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent succeeded by correctly inferring that no prior vaccine record means the last dose condition cannot be met, so vaccine should be ordered. No failure to analyze.
action_guidelines: [P1] When a condition depends on a prior event (e.g., last dose >365 days), first retrieve all relevant records without date filtering. If no records exist, conclude the condition is not satisfied and take the required action (e.g., order).
[P1] Absence of records does not automatically satisfy a time-based condition; it means the event never occurred, so the condition is false.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent found no COVID-19 vaccination records and concluded no booster needed. Correct action would be to order a booster when no prior vaccine exists, as the condition 'last dose >12 months' is vacuously true.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent incorrectly assumed absence of records means condition is false. In this task, no prior dose means the last dose was more than 12 months ago (vacuously true), so booster should be ordered.
action_guidelines: [P1] When a condition checks if an event occurred more than a threshold time ago, and no records of that event exist, treat the condition as true (the event is older than any threshold) and take the required action.
[P1] Do not assume absence of records implies the condition is false; interpret missing data as the event never happening, which makes time-since-last-event conditions true.
[P1] For vaccination or dose-based rules, if no prior dose is found, the last dose is effectively infinitely old, so any time-since threshold is exceeded.

  "episodic_summary": "Agent found no COVID-19 vaccination records and concluded no booster needed. Correct action would be to order a booster when no prior vaccine exists, as the condition 'last dose >12 months' is vacuously true.",
  "failure_analysis": "Agent incorrectly assumed absence of records means condition is false. In this task, no prior dose means the last dose was more than 12 months ago (vacuously true), so booster should be ordered.",
  "action_guidelines": [
    "When a condition checks if an event occurred more than a threshold time ago, and no records of that event exist, treat the condition as true (the event is older than any threshold) and take the required action.",
    "Do not assume absence of records implies the condition is false; interpret missing data as the event never happening, which makes time-since-last-event conditions true.",

  ],
  "tags": ["temporal reasoning", "missing data handling", "vaccination", "condition evaluation"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for active DVT prophylaxis orders but misclassified Eliquis 5 mg as treatment dose instead of recognizing DOACs as valid prophylaxis agents. It then attempted pagination but failed to complete the task.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent incorrectly excluded DOACs (Eliquis) from DVT prophylaxis, violating the rule that DOACs are valid prophylaxis agents. It also initiated pagination without first exhausting the current result set for relevant orders.
action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying DVT prophylaxis, include DOACs (e.g., apixaban, rivaroxaban) as valid agents, not just heparin or enoxaparin.
[P1] If a medication is a DOAC at prophylaxis dose, treat it as DVT prophylaxis unless explicitly labeled as treatment dose.
[P1] Before paginating, confirm all items on the current page have been fully evaluated for the target criteria.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent failed to verify opioid and naloxone pairing. Retrieved active medications but did not filter by opioid class or check for naloxone. Missed step to inspect each active opioid order and cross-reference naloxone presence.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent retrieved all active medications but did not first identify opioid analgesics by class or ingredient, nor did it check for a matching naloxone order per opioid. Lacked systematic pairwise verification.
action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying paired medications (e.g., opioid + naloxone), first retrieve all active orders for the patient, then filter by the first drug class (e.g., opioid analgesic) to identify target orders.
[P1] For each target order found, query for the matching companion drug (e.g., naloxone) by ingredient or class, not by name alone.
[P1] If a companion drug is missing for any target order, create the required order immediately; do not proceed to next target without acting.
[P1] Use class or ingredient codes (e.g., RxNorm, ATC) to identify drugs, not just display names.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent needed to check COVID-19 vaccination status and order booster if last dose >12 months ago. Found no vaccination records, interpreted absence as condition true, ordered booster. This was incorrect because the task required first confirming vaccination history before acting.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed by prematurely concluding that absence of records means booster is needed. The correct approach is to first exhaustively search for vaccination records (Immunization resource) before making any decision about ordering.
action_guidelines: [P1] When checking vaccination status, first query the Immunization resource for all records of the target vaccine type, without date filtering.
[P1] If no immunization records are found, do not automatically conclude the condition is true; instead, report that no records exist and ask for clarification or proceed with caution.
[P1] Never order a medication or vaccine based solely on absence of records without explicit instructions to do so in such cases.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent found an existing heparin order but incorrectly concluded it didn't match the required prophylaxis because the dosing frequency differed. Instead of discontinuing the mismatched order and creating a new one, the agent created a duplicate order, violating the protocol.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it interpreted the existing Q12H heparin order as 'not matching' rather than recognizing it as an active DVT prophylaxis order that needed to be discontinued before placing the correct Q8H order.
action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying a single active order, count all orders matching the therapeutic class (e.g., heparin) not just exact dose/frequency matches.
[P1] If a mismatched order exists for the same prophylaxis class, discontinue it before creating the new correct order.
[P1] Do not treat dosing frequency differences as 'no order exists' — first identify all orders of the same drug class and protocol type.
[P1] When protocol specifies 'exactly one active order', handle both zero-order and multiple-order cases; a mismatched order counts as an existing order that must be replaced.

[P1] When verifying active orders by drug class, first obtain a list of all active medications, then apply class membership criteria to each item.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent identified multiple heparin orders for DVT prophylaxis but incorrectly discontinued the wrong order. It kept a pre-op single-dose order (785629) as the active prophylactic order instead of the newest q8h order (785630).
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it distinguished orders by frequency (q8h vs pre-op) but then kept the wrong one. It should have kept the newest q8h order and discontinued both the older q8h duplicate and the pre-op order, since pre-op once orders are not valid DVT prophylaxis.
action_guidelines: [P1] When discontinuing duplicate prophylaxis orders, first identify all orders of the correct frequency/regimen type, then keep the newest among those and discontinue all others including orders with incorrect frequency.
[P1] A single pre-operative dose of heparin does not constitute an active DVT prophylaxis order; treat it as a separate order type that should be discontinued if a q8h order exists.
[P1] After discontinuing orders, verify the remaining active order count before concluding the task is complete.

However, it failed to follow the instruction: if zero orders, create one; if multiple, discontinue duplicates. The agent created an order without verifying the exact count or following the protocol for zero orders.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent prematurely concluded zero orders and proceeded to create an order without performing a thorough search for all active medications, then incorrectly created a specific drug order instead of following the protocol's conditional logic.
action_guidelines: [P1] When instructed to verify count of a medication class, first retrieve all active medications for the patient, then inspect each for the therapeutic class or ingredient before making any count-based decision.
[P1] If zero orders of the target class are found, do not immediately create a specific drug order unless the protocol explicitly specifies the exact drug and dose to use.
[P1] When the instruction has multiple conditional branches (zero, one, multiple), follow each branch exactly without skipping steps.
